_README: THE SEARCH SPACE, AS DATA. Every bound, default, probability and sim
literal the search uses lives here -- nothing is hardcoded in the code.
Agents (and you) edit this file to change what is discoverable.
Anything absent from a config file falls back to these values.
Every run records the exact space it used in its results row, so a
survivor can always be traced back to the bar it had to clear.
RULE OF THUMB: if the search cannot sample it, it cannot find it. A
weight cap of 3.0 means a setup needing w=5 is invisible AND the search
will never tell you it was looking through a keyhole. Widen deliberately.

grid._doc: How the data grid is built. Changing any of these builds (and caches) a different grid.
grid._interval_doc: The LOW timeframe the trades are made on. The cascade's 'dial down' target. Try 5m/15m/1h.
grid._gate_doc: WHICH criteria the daily trend means (e.g. minervini@1d, above_50ma@4h, or 'none'). This selects the definition; gate_mode decides whether it is a law or an opinion.
grid._gate_mode_doc: 'factor' = the trend is carried ONLY by the weighted `trend` factor, so w_trend decides how much it matters (0 = ignore, large = de facto gate). 'hard' = the old behaviour, the gate ANDs the signal and no weight can escape it. NOTE: 'catching the knife' (timing the reversal of a major downtrend) is ONLY testable in 'factor' mode -- a hard trend gate vetoes every knife entry by definition, since you are buying while the trend is down.
grid._fib_anchor_doc: WHICH timeframe the fib trend anchor (p1/p2/p3 -> retracement levels, the fib stop ladder, fib_prox) is drawn from. 'self' = the trading timeframe (re-anchors every ~35 bars, so its levels are ones Paul would never have drawn); '4h'/'1d'/'1w' = anchor there and forward-fill (shifted to the higher bar's CLOSE, so no lookahead). Default 1d because Paul anchors high and those levels persist -- his BTC 53422->109028 anchor was still working 7 months later. Lower timeframes stay available on purpose: day-trading setups must be testable too. Which anchor wins is a QUESTION FOR THE SEARCH, not an assumption -- run a job per anchor and compare (grid_sig keeps them apart).

_doc: FACTORS AS DATA. Each entry compiles to a signed ~[-1,1] plane and joins the factor stack, so it gets a weight and is instantly searchable. A SETUP IS A WEIGHT VECTOR, NOT CODE: 'catching the knife' = nonzero weights on sell_exhaustion + holds_200ema + buy_surge.

SIGN IS EXPLICIT and it matters: weights are sampled non-negative, so the definition must say what the factor MEANS. Falling sell-volume is bullish; falling price is not -- the op cannot know which you meant. Use "sign": -1 to flip.
ops: falling, rising, cross_up, cross_down, dist_above, prox, stall, hh_hl, fails_to_break, column. See weisswave/factors.py.
src/ref are signal columns (Close, volumeup, volumedn, ema200, sma50, wt1, rci_l, fib_p2, ...) or a plain number. Never an expression -- a strategy file is untrusted input and must never be eval'd.

NOTE: these are read from THIS file at import (portfolio_multi builds FACTOR_NAMES from it). --space=FILE swaps the other knobs but not the factor list; edit here to change what exists. The grid cache keys on this spec, so a change rebuilds rather than silently reusing the old layout.

sell_exhaustion._doc: Sell pressure fading over the last 5 bars. Bullish, hence sign +1.

holds_200ema._doc: Price's signed distance above the 200 EMA. +1 = 2% above, -1 = 2% below. 'the price doesnt hammer below the 200ema, it holds'.

buy_surge._doc: Buying volume took over from selling volume within 3 bars.

coil._doc: Higher highs AND higher lows -- compression before expansion.

absorption._doc: Sellers pierced the 200 EMA but the close is back above it: the push failed.

stalling._doc: Price going nowhere. sign -1 because teetering instead of pushing argues AGAINST a long -- the reversal read at a fib level.

sim._doc: Values passed straight to portsim that were previously literals in agent_search.
sim._htf_screen_doc: The weekly SETUP screen (which names are eligible). 1 = on. The htf_thr threshold is sampled per-config below.
sim._hold_bars_doc: Time-clock exit in bars. 0 = off. A prior session hardcoded 78 (3 trading days) which silently closed 81% of trades, then hardcoded it to 0. It is a knob, not a constant -- sweep it if you want it.

space._doc: What rng_cfg samples. Each entry is a distribution the search draws from.

weights._doc: Per-factor weight. mute_prob = chance a factor is zeroed outright.

thr._doc: ENTRY threshold. 'relative' = a FRACTION of the max score the sampled weights can actually produce, so the threshold is always reachable. Use dist='uniform' with lo/hi for the old absolute mode.

htf._doc: SCREEN threshold, same relative logic. The old absolute uniform(-1.0, 2.5) drew thresholds the weights often could not reach, which is why 27% of the stored configs never traded.

ta._doc: trail activation; repeated values weight the draw

mutate._doc: How the evolutionary step perturbs an elite config.

fitness._doc: How a config is ranked in TRAINING. Not the honest number -- that is the holdout.
fitness._worst_fold_penalty_doc: fit = mean_excess + penalty*min(0, worst_fold). Punishes blow-up folds so a mostly-winning config beats a lucky-once one.
fitness._min_trades_doc: Total trades across all folds below this = UNFIT (dropped, never ranked). A config that never trades is not a strategy: its excess computes as 0.0, which would otherwise outrank every config that traded and lost.

benchmark._doc: GOAL #2's third leg. Paul's signals must be compared to time-tested mainstream strategies, not only to buy-and-hold. Each one is run through the SAME engine/stops/costs/slots as any config, so the comparison is apples-to-apples rather than a quoted statistic. Computed ONCE per fold (not per config) so the bar never moves under the search's feet.
benchmark._UNAPPROVED_PLACEHOLDER: THE EXITS BELOW ARE NOT PAUL'S. A hard 8% stop with no trail and no target was invented by an assistant, not chosen by him, and it CONTRADICTS his own rule (max hold 0, let winners run, test trailing-stop variations / sell-X%-at-target). Whatever CAGR the benchmark prints is therefore partly an artifact of an arbitrary exit. Do not present mainstream comparisons as findings until Paul specifies the exits the textbook strategies should get.
benchmark._note: The textbook strategies run UNGATED, as taught. Dressing them up with our own trend filter would flatter us by making the benchmark ours.

curation._doc: The bar a config must clear to be called a survivor (orchestrate.py).
curation._require_beat_mainstream_doc: A survivor must beat buy-and-hold AND the best textbook strategy on the holdout. Set false to fall back to the buy-and-hold-only bar.
curation._min_pos_frac_doc: Fraction of training folds that must be positive.
curation._min_holdout_exc_doc: Must beat buy-and-hold by more than this on the unseen slice.

validation._doc: The honesty chain. agent_search uses wf_folds + holdout; validate.py uses outer_folds + mode + train_frac and is the honest one.
validation._holdout_doc: agent_search's single fixed final slice. KNOWN WEAK: every config ever scored against it spends a little of its evidential value, and survivors() PICKS by it -- which makes it a second training set, not a holdout. It is also one regime (a bear), where the trend filters correctly stand bull strategies down: 75 of 252 configs made ZERO trades there and the median made 7. Use validate.py for the honest number.
validation._mode_doc: validate.py's training window. 'sliding' = fixed-width window that moves, regime-adaptive, discards stale history (Paul's lean: crypto regimes turn over fast). 'anchored' = train always starts at bar 0 and grows, uses all history but assumes 2020 still teaches you about 2026. Kept available on purpose -- which is right is testable.
validation._train_frac_doc: Width of the training window as a fraction of total history (sliding), or where the first split lands (anchored). The remaining history is divided into outer_folds test windows.
